audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 1 Samuel chapter 21, first-pass restored draft
audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for Nob sanctuary, David's deceptive mission claim, holy bread and purity, Doeg's presence and ethnicity, Goliath's sword, flight to Gath, fear, feigned altered behavior, saliva imagery, and mental-health/disability stigma.

verses.restored: David came to Nob, to Ahimelech the priest. Ahimelech trembled to meet David and said to him, Why are you alone and no one with you?

David said to Ahimelech the priest, King commanded me a matter and said to me, Let no one know anything of matter for which I send you and which I commanded you. I have appointed young men to a certain place.
Now what is under your hand? Give into my hand five loaves of bread, or whatever is found.
Priest answered David and said, There is no ordinary bread under my hand, but there is holy bread, if young men have kept themselves from women.

David answered priest and said, Indeed women have been withheld from us as yesterday and third day when I went out. Vessels/bodies of young men are holy, though journey is ordinary; how much more today shall it be holy in vessel.
Priest gave him holy bread, because no bread was there except bread of Presence, removed from before YHWH to place hot bread on day it was taken.
A man from servants of Saul was there that day, detained before YHWH. His name was Doeg the Edomite, chief of shepherds belonging to Saul.
David said to Ahimelech, Is there not here under your hand spear or sword? For neither my sword nor my weapons did I take in my hand, because matter of king was urgent.

Priest said, Sword of Goliath the Philistine whom you struck in valley of Elah—look, it is wrapped in garment behind ephod. If you will take it for yourself, take it, for there is no other except it here. David said, There is none like it; give it to me.
David arose and fled that day from before Saul and came to Achish king of Gath.
Servants of Achish said to him, Is not this David king of land? Did they not answer about him in dances, saying, Saul struck his thousands and David his ten-thousands?
David set these words in his heart and was very afraid before Achish king of Gath.
He changed his reason/behavior in their eyes, acted as one out of his senses in their hand, scratched marks on doors of gate, and let saliva run down upon his beard.
Achish said to his servants, Look, you see a man behaving madly. Why do you bring him to me?
Do I lack madmen, that you brought this one to behave madly before me? Shall this one come into my house?

translation_notes: David deceives Ahimelech about a royal mission. The deception occurs under flight and fear but exposes the priestly community to lethal risk; survival context does not erase consequences.
The holy-bread discussion reflects ancient ritual purity and male military assumptions. It may not be used to shame sexuality, women, menstruation, bodies, or consensual relationships.
Ahimelech feeds a person in danger from restricted sacred food. Human need and hospitality stand within, not outside, holiness.
Doeg is identified as Edomite, but ethnicity does not explain his later violence. 'Edomite' must not become an ethnic slur or inherited moral category.
Goliath's sword becomes a survival weapon and a traumatic trophy. Sacred spaces should not normalize stockpiling weapons or turning past killing into devotional memory.
David flees to Gath, home region of Goliath. Refugees may cross enemy lines and use imperfect strategies to survive.

The phrase commonly rendered 'pretended madness' describes deliberately altered behavior. It must not mock psychosis, cognitive disability, neurodivergence, trauma responses, homelessness, or people who drool.
Achish's contemptuous words reflect stigma in the story; they are not Divine speech or ethical instruction.
David's fear is explicitly named. Fear does not cancel courage, faith, or human dignity.
Psalm superscriptions later connect this episode to prayer traditions, but those associations should not erase the chapter's deception, stigma, and danger.
safety_note: Do not use this chapter to shame mental illness, disability, drooling, altered behavior, ritual bodies, women, sexuality, refugees, or people who lie under coercive danger. David's survival strategy is not a comedy routine about 'crazy people.' Ahimelech's hospitality does not make him responsible for Saul's later massacre, and Doeg's ethnicity does not explain his choices.
